text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - POST-TREATMENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Head and Neck Oncology Program

ATTENDING: [Medical Oncology, Head and Neck]
VISIT TYPE: Office follow-up, post-treatment surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old Chinese-American female, never-smoker, with HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition), presenting for her first scheduled post-treatment follow-up visit approximately 6 weeks after completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen originally presented approximately 9 months ago with a 3-month history of right-sided throat discomfort and mild odynophagia, initially attributed to reflux by her primary care provider. ENT evaluation revealed a right base of tongue mass, biopsy of which confirmed moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong p16 positivity (>75% staining) and HPV-16 RNA ISH positivity. Staging workup including CT neck with contrast, PET/CT, and MRI oropharynx/neck confirmed T2N1M0 disease (3.1 cm primary, single 2.4 cm right level IIA nodal metastasis, no extranodal extension, no distant disease). Multidisciplinary tumor board recommended definitive concurrent chemoradiation as standard of care. She completed IMRT to 70 Gy in 35 fractions (SIB technique, bilateral neck elective fields included) with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks x2 cycles, finishing treatment approximately 6 weeks ago. Treatment was completed on schedule without interruption.

Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis beginning in week 5, necessitating PEG tube placement by gastroenterology for nutritional support. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck, grade 2 fatigue, and grade 1 xerostomia. She successfully completed both planned cycles of cisplatin at full dose without dose reduction. At the end-of-treatment visit (2 weeks ago), she had grade 3 mucositis that was just beginning to improve, was using the PEG tube exclusively for nutrition, and had lost approximately 11 lbs from her pre-treatment baseline.

Today she reports substantial improvement compared with the nadir of her treatment. She rates her throat pain as 2/10 at rest, 4/10 with swallowing - a dramatic improvement from the 8/10 pain she experienced during weeks 5-7 of radiation. She has discontinued all opioid analgesics and is managing with scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID, which she finds adequate. She is working with speech-language pathology and has begun oral trials: she is able to tolerate pureed and soft foods (yogurt, scrambled eggs, mashed potatoes, smoothies) and thin liquids without aspiration per SLP evaluation. She estimates she is getting approximately 40-50% of her daily calories orally and the remainder through the PEG tube with overnight Osmolite 1.5 at 80 mL/hr. She denies any PEG tube site problems, bleeding, or signs of infection.

She continues to note persistent dry mouth that is quite bothersome - she keeps a water bottle at all times and uses an artificial saliva spray (Biotene). She has noticeable skin hyperpigmentation of the bilateral anterior neck from radiation dermatitis, but the moist desquamation has fully healed and she has no open skin lesions.

She denies fever, chills, hemoptysis, new neck masses, shortness of breath, or unintentional weight loss beyond what was attributable to treatment. She has not yet returned to work but is feeling well enough to manage most activities of daily living independently. She lives with her husband, who has been her primary caregiver throughout treatment.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue improving; rates energy at approximately 60% of pre-treatment baseline. No fever. Appetite gradually returning.
- HEENT: Persistent xerostomia, grade 1-2. Mild residual odynophagia improving. Bilateral neck skin hyperpigmentation, no open wounds. Denies trismus, new neck lumps, or ear pain.
- Pulmonary: No cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea.
- GI: Mild nausea occasionally, no vomiting. PEG tube functional, no site complications. Bowel function normal.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild neck stiffness, performing home range-of-motion exercises.
- Neurologic: No new focal deficits. Denies tinnitus at this time (audiology follow-up scheduled).
- Endocrine: Continues levothyroxine for pre-existing hypothyroidism; dose unchanged during treatment; TSH check due.
- Psych: Mild anxiety about scan results but overall coping well with strong family support.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (already known, confirmed with patient):
1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, right base of tongue, T2N1M0 - currently on surveillance post-treatment
2. Hypothyroidism - on levothyroxine (pre-existing, potentially exacerbated by radiation)
3. Seasonal allergic rhinitis - on as-needed antihistamines

SOCIAL HISTORY:
High school music teacher, on medical leave during treatment. Never-smoker. Social alcohol use prior to diagnosis, currently abstaining. Lives with husband; adult children nearby. Chinese-American ethnicity. Good social support system throughout treatment course.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of head and neck cancer. No known hereditary cancer syndromes.

ALLERGIES:
NKDA (confirmed)

MEDICATIONS:
1. Levothyroxine 100 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
2. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO TID (scheduled, for residual throat pain)
3. Biotene oral spray PRN (xerostomia)
4. Sodium bicarbonate/saline oral rinses TID
5. Osmolite 1.5 via PEG tube, 80 mL/hr x 14 hours overnight
6. Loratadine 10 mg PO daily PRN (seasonal allergies)
7. Aquaphor ointment topically to neck PRN
8. Ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN nausea (prescribed at treatment end, used infrequently)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 14, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air
Weight: 127 lbs (pre-treatment baseline ~136 lbs; current net loss 9 lbs, approximately 6.6% of baseline body weight)
Height: 5'4"
Performance Status: ECOG 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out light work)

General: Alert, oriented, well-groomed. Appears somewhat fatigued but in no acute distress. Noticeably thinner in face compared to prior photos in chart.

HEENT:
- Oral cavity: Moderate xerostomia noted on exam with dry mucous membranes and reduced saliva pooling. Residual erythema of the soft palate and posterior oropharynx, grade 1 mucositis. No confluent pseudomembranes. No visible oral ulcers. Tongue appears mildly atrophic at the base; no discrete mass palpable on bimanual examination.
- Oropharynx: Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed at bedside by ENT colleague in joint visit - right base of tongue mucosa shows post-radiation erythema and edema but no discrete exophytic or ulcerative mass lesion visualized. Base of tongue appears markedly diminished in bulk compared to diagnostic laryngoscopy. No necrotic tissue.
- Neck: Bilateral anterior neck with diffuse hyperpigmentation in radiation field (post-radiation dermatitis sequelae). No open wounds, no moist desquamation. No induration. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy in levels I-V bilaterally. Previously palpable right level IIA node is no longer palpable. PEG tube site: intact, no erythema, no discharge, no peritoneal signs.
- Ears: External auditory canals clear bilaterally.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No stridor.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG tube site at left upper quadrant, site clean.
Extremities: No edema.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No new focal findings.

---

LAB RESULTS (reviewed from recent draws):
- CBC: WBC 5.8 k/uL, Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, improved from treatment nadir), Plt 224 k/uL
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 139, K 4.0, Cl 103, CO2 25, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.85 mg/dL (stable, GFR ~87; reassuring given 2 cycles cisplatin), Glucose 94, ALT 22, AST 18, Alk Phos 78, Total Bilirubin 0.5, Albumin 3.6 g/dL (mildly low, improving)
- TSH: 5.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated; possible radiation-induced hypothyroidism exacerbating pre-existing condition; will increase levothyroxine)
- Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal; supplementation considered)

---

IMAGING:
- Post-treatment PET/CT (12-week scan) is PENDING - scheduled for approximately 6 weeks from today (12 weeks post-treatment completion total). Today's visit falls at the 6-week post-treatment mark; per NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive OPSCC, definitive response assessment PET/CT is recommended at 12 weeks post-treatment. We have reviewed no new imaging today.
- Last imaging reviewed: End-of-treatment documentation. Clinical examination today consistent with significant response to therapy (no palpable disease).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old woman with HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0), who has completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation (70 Gy IMRT + cisplatin x2 cycles) approximately 6 weeks ago. She is recovering on the expected trajectory. Key issues:

1. DISEASE STATUS - RESPONSE ASSESSMENT PENDING:
Clinical examination today is highly encouraging - no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy, and flexible nasopharyngoscopy shows no visible residual mass. This is consistent with an excellent early clinical response. However, formal response assessment will be deferred until the 12-week post-treatment PET/CT, which is standard of care for HPV-positive OPSCC and is scheduled. Given the excellent prognosis of this disease category (p16-positive, N1, no extranodal extension, ECOG 0 at presentation), her trajectory is very promising. Patient counseled on the plan to wait for the 12-week PET/CT before making any definitive conclusions about response.

2. ACUTE TREATMENT TOXICITIES - RESOLVING:
- Mucositis: Improving from grade 3 to grade 1. Pain substantially improved; opioid analgesics successfully discontinued. Continue acetaminophen scheduled. Continue oral rinses. Anticipate full mucosal healing over the next 4-8 weeks.
- Radiation dermatitis: Resolved to post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation; no active desquamation. Aquaphor as needed, sun protection to neck.
- Fatigue: Grade 1-2, improving. Encourage graduated return to activity and light exercise as tolerated.
- Dysphagia/nutritional: Oral intake advancing via SLP guidance. Currently ~50% oral, 50% tube feeds. Continue current tube feeding regimen. Goal to advance oral intake over coming weeks. PEG tube to remain in place until oral intake consistently meets nutritional needs (target >85% oral). Follow up with SLP in 2 weeks.

3. XEROSTOMIA:
Grade 1-2 xerostomia expected post-IMRT despite parotid sparing (mean doses 28 Gy right, 24 Gy left). Continue Biotene spray and frequent water sipping. Trial pilocarpine 5 mg TID to stimulate residual salivary function - patient counseled on side effects (diaphoresis, GI upset). Dental hygiene with fluoride trays critical to prevent radiation caries; confirm dental follow-up.

4. THYROID FUNCTION - RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM:
TSH 5.8 mIU/L on current levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. Given radiation field encompassing thyroid bed and known risk of radiation-induced hypothyroidism, levothyroxine dose increased to 125 mcg daily. Recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks. Endocrinology co-management to be arranged if dose adjustment proves complex.

5. CISPLATIN-RELATED SURVEILLANCE:
- Renal: Creatinine stable at 0.85 mg/dL, GFR ~87 mL/min. Reassuring. Monitor with each visit.
- Ototoxicity: Audiogram scheduled with audiology; baseline audiogram completed pre-treatment showed normal hearing. High-frequency monitoring per ASHA cisplatin ototoxicity guidelines.
- Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL - start magnesium oxide 400 mg PO BID and recheck with next labs.

6. SURVEILLANCE PLAN:
Post-treatment imaging: PET/CT whole body at 12 weeks post-treatment (scheduled, approximately 6 weeks from today). Per NCCN Head & Neck SCC guidelines for HPV-positive OPSCC, this PET/CT represents the preferred first response assessment modality. Clinical H&P every 1-3 months for years 1-2 per NCCN surveillance schedule. Annual CT neck with contrast for ongoing locoregional surveillance (or as clinically indicated). Annual thyroid function testing given radiation exposure.

7. FUNCTIONAL REHABILITATION:
- Continue SLP for dysphagia rehabilitation. Swallowing therapy exercises ongoing.
- Physical therapy referral for neck range-of-motion and lymphedema prevention.
- Psychosocial support: Social work referral offered; patient currently coping well with family support but resources discussed.

8. RETURN TO WORK:
Patient anticipates returning to part-time teaching duties within 4-6 weeks as energy improves and oral intake advances. Supported and encouraged; return to professional activities associated with improved quality of life in OPSCC survivors.

Return to clinic in 4 weeks (or sooner if concerns), and then for PET/CT result review at 12-week mark. Specific instructions given to contact clinic immediately for new neck mass, significant worsening of pain, fever, dyspnea, or any acute concerns.

---

Time spent: 45 minutes, >50% counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed - Medical Oncology, Head and Neck Program]
